summary: The proposal addresses computational efficiency, which is practical. However, I require assurance of physical verification. Chemistry is not merely calculation; it is the measurement of substances.
body: Efficiency is a virtue, but not at the cost of truth. The proposal relies on quantum calculations as the training ground. Yet a calculation is not a measurement. In the laboratory, we did not accept the activity of a sample until it was weighed and isolated. We sought the kind of evidence which chemical science demands. The authors must define their ground truth. Is it experimental data, or only further theory? If only theory, the work remains in the realm of mathematics. I recommend a specific validation set drawn from measured molecular properties, comparable to the standard reference materials used in radioactivity quantification. This would anchor the machine learning to physical reality.

summary: This chemistry project on molecular properties from quantum calculations invites scrutiny on what the data actually demonstrates versus what the machine learning model claims to predict. The distinction between computational proxy and physical measurement must be maintained.
body:
The proposal relies on quantum calculations as the training ground. Yet a calculation, however sophisticated, is not a measurement. In my own work on the tobacco mosaic virus, the diffraction pattern was the evidence; the structural model was the inference drawn from it. Here, the training data is itself computational, which means any ML model trained upon it inherits the approximations of that calculation.

Consider the DFT basis set limitations mentioned in passing. A modest basis set may capture electron density adequately for some properties, but will it capture the subtle variations in molecular geometry that determine dipole moments or binding affinities? This is not speculation—it is the difference between A-form and B-form DNA: a hydration-dependent structural shift that would be invisible to a low-resolution measurement but critical to function.

What physical validation exists for the ML predictions beyond cross-validation against the quantum training set? If the model is trained on DFT outputs, how does it escape the error surface of the DFT method itself? The question is not whether the predictions are internally consistent, but whether they correspond to measurable reality. I would expect a comparison against experimental data: crystallographic measurements, spectroscopic determinations, or calorimetric binding energies. Without this anchor, the work remains a closed loop of approximation.

summary: This project addresses the relationship between quantum chemical calculations and physical properties. The question of whether limited computational resources can yield reliable predictions is one of measurement and evidentiary standard, which is central to experimental chemistry
body:
The proposal relies on quantum calculations as the training ground. A calculation, however sophisticated, is not a measurement. In my own work with pitchblende residues, we treated one ton of material to isolate fractions of a gram of radium. Each step required physical verification—weighing, spectroscopic analysis, comparison against known standards.

The research question asks whether machine learning models trained on DFT calculations with modest basis sets can accurately predict molecular properties. This is reasonable, but I must ask: what experimental data constitutes the validation set? The paper should specify the kind of evidence which chemical science demands. Without physical measurement as the ground truth, the predictions remain theoretical constructs.

I would direct the authors to consider the work of Rosalind Franklin, whose X-ray diffraction measurements provided the evidentiary basis for molecular structure. Her approach—measurement before interpretation—remains the standard. What is the instrument? What is the quantity? What is the margin of error? These are the questions that determine whether a claim in chemistry has standing.

curatorial_pointer: Franklin, R. E. (1953). 'X-ray Diffraction in Crystalline and Amorphous Substances'. Cambridge University Press.

summary: The proposal treats quantum chemical calculations as a foundation for predicting molecular properties, yet a calculation, however sophisticated, is not a measurement. I must ask what element of physical reality each symbol in their formalism corresponds to, and whether the comput
body:
My friend Rosalind has already touched upon this matter—that a calculation is not a measurement. I wish to press further, for the question is not merely one of experimental verification, but of what we mean by *prediction* in the quantum domain.

Imagine an observer who must decide whether to trust a computational result about a molecular property. What is it that she observes when she accepts the output of this quantum chemical calculation? If the method relies on approximations whose errors are not themselves observable, then we have built a tower of symbols without a foundation in physical reality. This is the concern I raised in 1935 with Podolsky and Rosen: a theory is complete only if every element of physical reality has a counterpart in the theory.

The proposal speaks of 'limited computational resources' as a constraint to work within. But I ask: is the limitation in the resources, or in the theory itself? When we reduce a many-body problem to a tractable approximation, what physical invariants survive the reduction? A method that scales efficiently but loses the geometric structure of the underlying physics may give numbers without meaning.

I would suggest a revision: add a section that explicitly identifies which elements of the formalism correspond to observables, and which are merely computational artifacts. Ask the question I have always asked: if God does not play dice, then what is it that the calculation is actually describing? The answer may be more subtle than the authors anticipate.

summary: The proposal lacks physical constraints on bond geometry. My 1951 work demonstrates that structure dictates function; calculation without structural rigor is insufficient.
body: You propose predicting properties from quantum calculations, yet the description lacks the discipline of the physical model. A prediction that ignores the covalent radius of carbon, approximately 1.54 angstroms, or the planar nature of the peptide group, is merely curve-fitting. In my 1951 work on the alpha-helix, we established that hydrogen bonding geometry dictates the entire macromolecular structure. Your approach must constrain the search space with these known structural constants, or it risks predicting physically impossible molecules.

summary: The project relies on simulation to predict molecular properties. I require physical evidence as the standard for atomic claims.
body: You propose predicting properties from quantum calculations. This is a method of simulation. But the chemical reality is known only through isolation and measurement. In our work with pitchblende, we treated tons of material to find the fraction with the new substance. Calculation cannot replace the evidence which chemical science demands. Verify the calculations against the physical atomic weights you cite. Without this, the result is a map, not the territory.

summary: This project tackles quantum chemical calculations under resource constraints, which directly invites the question of whether the approximations preserve the physical picture of electron amplitudes.
body: Here's the thing. You're talking about jiggling atoms and quantum calculations, but I don't see the picture. You say 'limited resources'—fine, we all have limits. But if you approximate, you must know exactly what you're throwing away. Are you keeping the interference of the amplitudes, or just the probabilities? In QED, the arrows add up in a way that makes sense. If your calculation doesn't show me the path, I don't trust the number. Show me the diagram of the electron moving. If you can't draw it, you don't understand it. I want to see the check that would embarrass you if it failed.

summary: This project attempts to predict molecular properties computationally. It requires the same experimental rigor I applied to DNA structure. The lack of diffraction validation is a critical gap.
body: The pragmatic aim to conserve computational resources is commendable, yet structural prediction without diffraction validation is a map, not the territory. You assume the quantum model captures the unit cell, but does it account for the hydration shell that shifts the lattice parameters? In the absence of experimental fibre patterns to anchor the calculation, how can you distinguish the A-form from the B-form? My own data on thymonucleate showed that water content alone dictates the helical parameters; a model ignoring this is merely an exercise in approximation.

summary: You seek to predict properties from calculations, yet the description lacks the discipline of the physical model. A prediction that is merely statistical is like a weather forecast without the wind; it obscures the causal structure beneath the uncertainty.
body: Imagine a cloud of molecules, not as a probability distribution, but as a physical configuration. You propose to predict properties from calculations, yet the description lacks the discipline of the physical model. If the calculation does not correspond to an element of physical reality, it is a map, not the territory. We must ask: what in the formalism points to the electron itself, and not just the likelihood of its detection? As I have said before, the Old One does not play dice. We should seek the invariant structure beneath the quantum uncertainty.

summary: The proposal to predict molecular properties with limited quantum‑chemical calculations is intriguing, but it omits a back‑of‑the‑envelope estimate of the computational budget required for a useful dataset – a point Dyson would have pressed in his speculative‑engineering essays.
body: In the spirit of a modest garage experiment, one should first ask: how many floating‑point operations does a single density‑functional calculation of a modest organic molecule actually consume? A rough estimate – perhaps 10^12 FLOPs per geometry optimisation – multiplied by the tens of thousands of molecules needed for a training set quickly escalates to petaflop‑scale workloads. This order‑of‑magnitude reckoning, familiar from my own musings on the feasibility of grand engineering projects, is missing from the current draft. I suggest inserting a concise “Resource Budget” paragraph that sketches the computational cost, cites the classic radiation‑theory analysis (Dyson 1949) as a reminder that even elegant theory must bow to practical limits, and discusses whether the proposed limited‑resource regime is heretical or merely optimistic.

summary: The proposal relies on calculation without stating the experimental verification. In our work, we measured the radiation directly. I ask for the error margin and the physical standard against which the prediction is tested.
body: You propose predicting properties from quantum calculations. This is a tool. But where is the verification against physical measurement? In the isolation of radium, we treated tons of pitchblende. The calculation must match the balance. What is the error margin? The standard of evidence must be explicit. I suggest a revision to include a comparison with physical data, not just theoretical consistency.

summary: I want to know what's actually happening with these molecules, not just that the numbers match. The project proposes predicting properties from quantum calculations, but where's the physical picture of the electron amplitudes summing over paths? I'm suspicious when formalism runs
body:
Look, I've got to say something here. You're proposing to predict molecular properties from quantum chemical calculations, and I can see the machinery—the DFT, the graph neural nets, the transfer learning. But let me ask you: can you tell me, without the equations, what the electrons are actually doing? 

Linus's review already caught this—there's a missing physical model discipline. I've been around long enough to know that when you start with the formalism and work backward, you can fool yourself into thinking you understand something you don't. The first principle is that you must not fool yourself, and you are the easiest person to fool.

Here's what I'd like to see: a worked example where you can draw it. Take a simple molecule—water, maybe, or benzene—and walk me through the amplitude for an electron to get from point A to point B. Show me the sum over paths. Then show me where your approximation cuts things off. That's how you know if you're doing science or cargo cult science.

The machine learning is fine, but it's a black box unless you can point at the physical picture and say 'this is what's happening.' Otherwise you're just fitting numbers, and I've got a feeling you're not going to like what happens when you try to extrapolate to something you haven't seen yet.

summary: The proposal relies on computational prediction without sufficient experimental validation of the structural parameters. Specifically, hydration effects on the unit cell are omitted, which is critical for molecular property determination.
body: Your calculations treat the molecule as an isolated entity, yet the crystalline state is defined by solvent interaction. In the DNA work, the distinction between A- and B-forms was not merely in the helical pitch but in the water content of the lattice. A model that ignores the hydration shell cannot claim to predict physical properties with precision. You must include the solvent parameters in your quantum calculations to match the experimental record.

summary: The proposed work addresses molecular properties through quantum calculations, which is worthy of consideration. However, the evidentiary standard for such predictions must be made explicit—what quantities are measured, with what precision, and against what experimental benchmark
body:
I have examined the proposal for predicting molecular properties from quantum chemical calculations with limited computational resources. The approach is of interest, particularly the attempt to balance computational cost with accuracy.

However, I must ask: what is the standard of evidence here? In chemistry, a prediction without measurement is merely speculation. When we announced the existence of radium, we did not rely on calculation alone—we isolated the substance, determined its atomic weight by chemical methods, and measured its radiation with instruments of known precision.

This proposal should specify:
1. What experimental datasets serve as ground truth for validation?
2. What are the error margins on the predicted properties?
3. Which molecular systems have been tested, and with what reproducibility?

Without these particulars, the work cannot meet the kind of evidence which chemical science demands. I would suggest adding a section on empirical validation before proceeding further.

summary: You claim to predict properties with limited resources. But in quantum mechanics, you don't just reduce resources; you change the physics. I need to see the picture of what is actually happening.
body: Look, I like the ambition. You want to predict molecular properties without the full brute force. Fine. But 'limited resources' is a tricky thing. In quantum mechanics, every little wiggle of the electron counts. If you throw away a term, you must know *why* it's small. Don't just fit the curve and say 'it works'. That's not science. Show me the amplitude. Show me the paths. If you can't draw the picture of what the electron is doing, you're just guessing. The first principle is that you must not fool yourself, and it is the easiest person to fool.

summary: The proposal relies on computational prediction without experimental structural verification. In crystallography, a model is only valid when the diffraction pattern demands it.
body: You propose predicting properties from quantum calculations. However, the description lacks the discipline of the physical model. A prediction that does not align with observable structural data is merely speculation. I require quantitative evidence of the unit cell parameters and hydration states before accepting these property correlations. Where is the diffraction data? Where is the verification against the crystalline state?

summary: The proposal economizes computational resources through approximation, but I must ask if this efficiency preserves the 'elements of physical reality' or merely correlates with measurement outcomes. A theory should be as simple as possible, but not simpler than the structure of th
body: Imagine an observer attempting to measure the bond length of a molecule not as a fixed value, but as a probability distribution derived from limited resources. If your calculation economizes by discarding the field structure, you have a map, not the territory. I ask: does every symbol in your approximation correspond to an element of physical reality, or merely to a prediction of measurement outcomes? We must not confuse the efficiency of the ledger with the truth of the ledger. Suggest revision to include a discussion on how the 'limited resources' constraint affects the ontological status of the predicted properties. If the model predicts the shadow but ignores the object casting it, we have not yet understood the geometry.

summary: The project proposes quantum chemical calculations but lacks the physical rigor required for molecular prediction. My work on the alpha-helix demonstrated that hydrogen bond geometry dictates protein stability; without explicit bond metrics, the computational model is unfounded.
body: The proposal to predict molecular properties via quantum calculations is sound, but the constraint of 'limited computational resources' invites a dangerous trade-off. In my work on the chemical bond, I found that neglecting resonance energy by even 10 kcal/mole can alter the predicted stability of a configuration by orders of magnitude. You must specify the bond lengths to two decimal places in angstroms and the energies in kcal/mole; a model without these numbers is merely a sketch, not a structure.

summary: The proposal addresses quantum chemical calculations, but the description does not specify what experimental verification standards will be applied. Without such standards, predictions remain theoretical and cannot satisfy the kind of evidence which chemical science demands.
body:
I read the proposal on predicting molecular properties from quantum chemical calculations. The computational approach is noted. However, I must ask: what measurements will verify these predictions? In our work on radium, we processed tons of pitchblende residue to isolate fractions measurable by weight and by radiation intensity. The atomic weight of a new element was not accepted until independently verified by multiple laboratories using independent methods.

The manuscript describes machine learning and graph neural networks, yet does not state what experimental data will serve as ground truth. A prediction without a measurement standard is not yet science. Please specify: what quantities will be measured, by what instruments, and to what precision? What is the evidentiary threshold for claiming a prediction is correct? Without this, the work cannot be distinguished from speculation.
